flashcards.front: What is the fundamental difference between P and NP classes?
flashcards.back: P contains decision problems solvable in polynomial time, while NP contains problems verifiable in polynomial time.
A problem is NP-Hard if every NP problem can be reduced to it in polynomial time.
A problem is NP-Complete if it's both in NP and NP-Hard (all NP problems reduce to it).
Proved SAT is NP-Complete, establishing first NP-Complete problem and foundation for complexity theory.

flashcards.front: What's the practical implication if P=NP?
flashcards.back: Many currently intractable problems would have efficient solutions, revolutionizing cryptography and optimization.
1. Boolean Satisfiability (SAT) 2. Traveling Salesman 3. Graph Coloring
Converting problem A to problem B in polynomial time such that solution to B solves A.

flashcards.front: Is every NP-Hard problem in NP?
flashcards.back: No. NP-Hard problems may be harder than NP (not necessarily verifiable in polynomial time).

flashcards.front: What's the difference between decision and optimization problems?
flashcards.back: Decision problems ask yes/no questions, optimization seeks best solution. NP classes focus on decision versions.
It's in NP (solutions verifiable in polynomial time) and can reduce to/from other NP-Complete problems.

flashcards.front: What's the role of certificates in NP problems?
flashcards.back: Certificates are proposed solutions that can be verified in polynomial time.

flashcards.front: Can approximation algorithms solve NP-Hard problems exactly?
flashcards.back: No - they provide near-optimal solutions but don't guarantee exact answers in polynomial time.
